sentences.text: Well, I'll go back.
Before I said that I'm going-- why we left that part.
Because in that part, was very bad-- started getting very bad.
At one time, I went with a friend of mine just to ask for some bread, or anything like that.
Whatever they would give us, we were have a happy.
And when he went into the inn, I stayed outside.
They opened the door.
They put on   the threshold, and they chopped his head off with an axe.
And they kicked his head away.
And so I kept running.
Another incident was that I went inside-- not him, just I went in to ask for some bread.
And the guy wanted to kill me, too.
And another one jumped in, and he said, oh, don't touch him, that's Gold's son.
So I was survived.
There's another incident that I can remember that I went into a different place, and I said that I was a Ukrainian.
And they said, what do you mean Ukrainian?
Where are you from?
And I said to them, from a village that the Germans burned down.
They didn't burn the rest of the village.
They took the people, and they put them into a big-- big place.
And they blocked up the doors and the windows, and they put it on fire.
And I says, I ran away.
I don't know if my parents are alive.
And they gave me lots of bread.
They put a whole bag with bread, with all different things.
I says, I have to go back to see if I can see my mother or anybody who survived.
We had a feast that time.
This what happened in there before.
Ukrainians.
All Ukrainians.
No, there was-- in the village, there were no Polish people there.
All there was just Ukrainians.
Because the Ukrainians-- if there was any Polish people there, they were killing the Polish.
They wanted to have a clean [NON-ENGLISH], national Ukraine.
And so the Polish people, most of them were gone already.
So that was only Ukrainian villages that we were in.
We were knew that we could not survive.
Every time, there was somebody else were killed.
And there was one time that I came back to the Russians, and I wanted to go into the same hole that I was in there.
But my mother was standing outside, and I wanted to go inside.
I says, I'll crawl in, see what it is.
And there was a wolf in there.
He occupied my spot.
So I had to go somewhere else to dig a  hole.
No communication.
If you met somebody, if he wanted to stick with you, he stuck with you.
If he saw that you will-- you will be a hazard to him, he just left you.
There was no communication.
There was a woman with two children.
We found their bones, eaten by the-- by the wolves.
So there was-- they wouldn't-- they wouldn't try to take you.
If they could run faster, they would run faster.
That-- that's how it was.
Survive on his own, everyone.
Yeah.
There-- there-- there was no deaths.
We stuck together.
We stuck together like say in the end.
The end, which when we crossed over, like I told you, And we met there the boy with the girl.
And the mother-- he told us, my mother froze to death.
So we went to that part.
And we already took a lot of food.
And there was some cabbage, and there was all kinds of stuff.
And we prepared it in that village.
And we dug underground, and we stayed there.
And we knew that it's very far from the Ukrainian villages.
Because it was far forest.
And it was not Ukrainian style, but it was Polish.
But the Polish left, so we were there.
And we were there-- well, we came there already at the end of September, probably, the end of the summer.
I don't remember the dates.
We came down to the summer to take up the stuff in the fields there.
A lot of them were shot.
Even the Ukrainian came there with a horse or anything like that, he was shot.
And they took-- we found some of the bodies.
If we could bury, we buried.
There was a rabbi's son that he was teaching me a little bit in the war, and to pray.
And he was shot afterwards.
There was others.
And one time, the Ukrainian came into the [NON-ENGLISH] And they were trying to.
So they killed a few, and we ran away further.
And all the time, that's how we stayed.
Oh, no-- the same clothes.
And even my mother, when the wintertime, we were for sure not.
But in the summertime, she used to take it off.
And she wanted to have it washed and everything like that.
And when I washed it-- and I was naked.
And when I put it back on, there was more lice than I had before.
And it was-- So I says, I don't have you wash my clothes anymore.
And, you know, I was-- no, it was not-- clothes, we had only one.
OK, We get into the winter of the '43, or '44-- I don't remember.
So there was incident.
The Ukrainian came to us again.
They wanted to kill us all.
And we only had one idea, just to run, to be shot, not to be caught.
Because they said a bullet is too expensive for a Jew.
To cut his throat, or took him  apart or-- They took one guy-- which I didn't see it, but they told me about it.
They took him one leg to one horse, one leg to the other, and they split him in half.
All kinds of things.
They took-- they cut his nose off, cut his ears off, you know, to see him suffer in different ways.
They took a saw and split him in half.
All kinds of things.
So we always ran away to get a bullet, to get everything else, but not to be caught.
When they came to us and they got, I ran away.
I had made from pieces of wood like-- like shoes.
Not shoes, just a piece of thing.
And I had from the skin of the tree, and they tied it up and rolled it around with a rag.
It was wintertime.
We started running.
I jumped on the ice, and I fell in up to my neck into the water.
I crawled out and I left my beautiful shoes under.
And I started running barefooted.
And it was better than on skates.
You can run.
Well, I ran till late at night.
Then I met a few people that were standing and hiding out there.
So they took off their hats and they gave me to put my feet into it.
My mother and my brother.
But all three of us were separated.
At that time when we were running, everybody for himself.
We'd lose everybody.
We'd lose just-- I'll tell you an incident, another one.
There was a little girl born in 1943.
The father grabbed the little daughter and he ran away with her.
And the mother was separate.
And the little girl didn't cry.
She didn't eat all day.
She had to be fed.
And she's living in New York.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And evening-- because they didn't kill nobody that time.
They wanted to take us all.
After a while, we heard a plane crossing our forest every single day.
All of a sudden, the plane stopped coming.
See, that's why I don't know when I was free.
And when the plane stopped crossing, we thought something was wrong.
That was a German plane.
So four guys went out from the forest.
And everybody was yelling not to go, because we were snowed in, not to make tracks.
When they did go, so they always covered tracks to cover up.
But they went out.
And the Germans left already.
The Russians were there already 10 days.
So the Russians took two guys with them.
And two they let go to come to tell us that the Russians were there.
So we left.
I go back to another incident.
The same time that I fell into the water, another guy fell in.
Not at the same place, it was somewhere else.
He was frozen.
And we carried him out from the bush.
The Russians took him in.
We never found him alive.
So we don't know if the Russians gave him something to die, but he was frozen all the way half down.
He was blue.
He was completely frozen.
I was not frozen.
I survived.
Holes underground to go under the frozen spot.
At the last place, we couldn't dig too far because it was a low land.
It was water coming in.
So we-- the only thing was to stand up, if you could see, somebody could see from far away.
See your head or anything like that.
We could not allow   to.
You had to go bending down.
You had to crawl.
So in the other forest, we dug underground.
We were under.
We just took-- just a few branches over it or anything like that.
And this was a [NON-ENGLISH].
Under there, in the low lands, we were already more-- Describe what it was like to be in these bunkers.
To be in the bunker?
There's not to describe, because it's a survival.
It's-- like you would say a dog's life, a dog has a good life here.
An animal?
I don't think so.
Because they were killing us more than animals.
Just lay down, put a little bit of leaves, and just lay on-- on the ground.
There's nothing I could say that-- what happened there.
There was all kinds of people.
There was couples.
There was the different people.
They dug out a little hole, and they lived together there.
And so me, and my mother, and my brother.
We were in our one hole, just the three of us.
Not very long.
If we felt that somebody knows about it or anything like that-- like we stayed in the bushes, in the one time before.
And the Ukrainians were going to make some vodka from the rye, or anything like that.
And we heard if somebody was coming.
Because they came to one-- one there, and they killed them all.
We heard that that was killed somebody, we rushed to another place.
We didn't know where is a safe place.
We never knew where we going to be safer, here or there.
But all the time on the move.
Because if we felt that somebody knows that we were there, we would go back further.
Not further than the bush.
Because if you went further than the bush, it's no good, because we got the animals.
And the first thing, we didn't have no food.
We had to be closer to the farm parts, to-- or even, we used to get out to the farmers, which they bury potatoes for the next year's crop.
And they cover it up.
And we used to go through the frost and all that to dig out a few potatoes to have a few days of potatoes to eat.
I was shot.
The only thing is for a few days, or a week-- I don't remember how long it took me-- I couldn't step on it.
And I was just laying there and crawling.
And whatever my mother did for me, there was nothing, [NON-ENGLISH].
Because I only was shot just a flesh wound.
It just took off some skin, which I have a mark there.
And my brother was worse off.
Because a lot of times he was-- when he was shot, he lost blood and he was drying.
He was shrinking.
You could tell then that he was.
But we didn't pay attention to wounds or-- I don't know, we were never sick.
I never caught a cold there.
And you stayed laying in the bush.
And in winter, it's as cold as in Canada here.
And sometimes the winter is very harsh there.
Nobody caught a cold.
Nobody had the flu.
In that underground, just a little bit of hay, a little bit of straw, or a few leaves, and you're rolling down, that's it.
If you made a fire, it was good.
Because a fire was also not good, because it's underground.
On top, you see the flame.
But on top, they can smell the smoke.
And they smell the smoke, they know exactly.
According to the smoke- they'll come and catch you.
So you see, that's why we are afraid.
Why we made the fire was also underground, we never made a fire on top.
On top, you see right away.
It shows far.
We learned how to live like-- to survive like.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This was in the forest.
And we came out from the forest.
When we came out, the Russians arrested us.
We said, what do you mean?
We are here 10 days, and you are still in the bush?
And the Ukrainians from the village had to come and tell them that they had Jewish people.
So it was the first run.
And we were not far.
We came to Malynska, to my hometown, and to my house.
And my house was a hospital, the first hospital of the Russian front.
And I begged them to let me in.
They wouldn't let me in.
They just gave me a little corner in the kitchen on the floor.
So they brought in a bunch of straw, put it on there, and we were sleeping in our own home, just in the corner.
But they gave us a little bit of food already, because we didn't have nothing.
I was ripped of clothes.
A Russian soldier gave me a little shirt, and he gave me a pair of pants, which they were big on me, and all that.
And I wore all Russian uniform and all that.
My mother got a little bit of  clothes from some of the Ukrainian neighbors.
They attitude to say, well, how come you are still alive?
How did you alive?
How Is this?
To them, was a shock.
What do you mean, we-- we still have Jews?
I though you were all massacred, they were all dead.
What are you-- the surprise-- you're living?
Pleased that we were-- they were-- first thing, we had-- then when the Russian front went further, some Ukrainians moved into our house.
We couldn't throw them out.
They wouldn't pay us nothing.
They wouldn't give us a room to sleep in.
Finally, we got them out, some of them, and we lived in the house.
And of course, then we had to make to live.
We didn't have the work to live, too.
You see, we-- I went to the mill.
And a Ukrainian who knew me-- knew my father, not me-- I begged him for a little bit of flour.
When they milled the wheat, or the corn, or anything else, they gave me a little bit.
And I filled up a bag of a few kilos of flour.
And I brought it home.
And my mother made some bread, and she sold some.
Or she gave something to trade for eggs, or trade for a chicken, and all that, Because money, you know?
And then we started accumulating a little bit of stuff from the women from the Ukraine.
We got some butter, and we got some pork, and we got some eggs.
And I used to get on the train and get to [NON-ENGLISH] or a different city and sell that, and buy salt, and buy some yeast, and drag it back in.
And that's how we started to switch.
We never went back to beg for flour.
And we used to switch already.
For a pound of yeast, we used to get a pound of pork, or a pound of butter.
And we used to get-- for a pound, we used to get-- for a small piece of yeast, we used to get a chicken or something, or a few eggs.
And that's how we-- my mother used to do it.
And I used to go there.
One time I went from there with some eggs.
I had about 60 eggs with me.
That was a lot.
And I had a couple of pounds of pork, and a few pounds of butter.
And another fellow went with me on the train, on the freight train.
We never went to the passenger, because we had a piece of paper false which we went in the post office.
There was a girl there, and she put a stamp on, and somebody signed it.
And there was no papers.
So identification-like.
And all that.
So we-- we went, the two of us.
We were going to [NON-ENGLISH] with the stuff to sell.
In the middle of the train starting moving, you jumped out of the train, and you went-- I don't know what happened to him.
It's like, I says, what happened to you?
He went home and he told my mother that he heard some of the people on the train-- because there were people even on top of the train.
There was freight inside, and outside, and on top of the roof light.
I was hooked on like this on the outside with the basket of eggs and everything.
And they said, we will throw those guys off in the middle of the road.
That's what my mother had to hear, that they were going to kill me.
But it never happened.
And I don't know, I never heard it.
He heard it.
I came into [NON-ENGLISH] and the train stopped.
And there was a train with army going to the front.
So they asked, what have I got?
I says, I've got eggs.
What kind of eggs?
Eggs.
Cooked?
I says, no.
Cook me one.
So I found some metal thing, I put some water, and I cooked the eggs right up front of the train.
I sold all the eggs, and I was cutting up pieces of butter.
They gave me-- I made a fortune that time.
They gave me everything.
If they didn't have money, they gave me clothes, they gave me everything.
We had money.
And I bought the eggs, and I bought some salt, and I bought yeast, and all that.
And I came back.
On the way back, I was gone already in a passenger train.
And a policeman comes over and he says, what have you got?
I said, I haven't got nothing, because I hid it underneath.
And then-- let me see your papers.
I show him the paper, he looks at the paper, he didn't like it.
OK, you'll come with me.
Where do you live?
And I told him a station further than Malynska.
The next station over Malynska.
So he says, OK.
When he was walking up and down, I was going with him and all that.
He said, no, no, no, you go back to sit down, I'll call you, and don't you go away.
That's all I wanted to hear.
Because all of the stuff that-- soon as we got to Malynska, he went to the other side.
And I took the stuff, and I went on the other side and under the train.
And I was gone.
Yeah, it was a lot of fun to make a living.
But that's how we stayed till about May the 1st, May Day.
In Malynska, from all of us-- oh, no, there was a lot of Jewish people.
I mean, a lot.
There wasn't just 60 people, there was about 25 or more.
But they were not Malynska people.
From Malynska survived-- oh, I think I had some time figured out it was seven of us.
But they were not all in there.
Because some of them, they took them away to the Russian Army.
After the war, we were small.
I was 15.
Yeah, I was 15 or 16 at that time.
And my brother was two years younger.
So that's what-- anybody over 18, they took away to the army already.
But we survived only from Malynska.
Came out from that forest where we came out, there came out 70 people.
But there were people from few hundred kilometers away.
There was one guy, he was from Chelm.
And Chelm was Poland.
It was never Ukrainian.
How he got to us, I-- we don't know how he ran through.
I never knew how Moniek came to us.
We didn't at that time.
We just-- and I know one thing.
When we left on the May of 1st, we went to Chelm.
Train stopped for the Chelm, we went to look for him.
And then we found some Polish people, they said they killed him.
He went to look for his parents if anybody survived.
They killed Moniek there.
In Chelm.
Yeah.
So Malynska people did not survive, too many.
Yeah.
There were only about six or seven that I know of.
And there were-- and like I said, there were many more living.
They lived within our house a lot.
Which they used to come from different towns to take the train.
So they used to stay in our house.
And a lot of them, they didn't want to stay in their hometowns, because a lot of them on the way to their hometown, they were killed by the Ukrainians.
After the war.
Oh, after the war, after the war.
This was after.
They were killed.
So they stayed, a lot of them.
I know-- have a lot of friends which they are not from Malynska.
But like I said, Malynska only survived about five or six from the-- what did I say, 65 to 70?
That's all we were.
Right now that I know of Malynska-- I don't know anybody else except me.
No.
At one time.
One time we were in the forest, and they came running.
And one guy said, the Germans are coming.
And we started digging somewhere to hide and everything else.
And that was the Russian partisans.
Why they said the Germans, because the Russian partisans, they were going big.
They had in front guys with uniform of Germans.
And everybody thought they was German.
They were screaming, and yelling, and they had motorcycle Germans, and then they came in.
But we wanted to join and all that.
Like actually, my brother was wanted to.
I didn't say nothing, because my mother.
And they said, they don't take women and children.
So that was one.
There was an incident that I want to forget.
Because one time, one Russian partisan says to me, come with me.
I didn't mention to you there.
I came with me, and I didn't want to go.
And he says, you have to go.
And he was drunk.
And he was going to dynamite the track.
And he went up to the track and carried pieces of soap, which I didn't know what it was.
But it was dynamite.
You put the capsule in, then-- he was drinking the vodka like without end.
He went up to put it up there.
And I was sitting in the bush far away from him.
I just went to carry for him.
And he ripped himself apart.
His head hit me over the head.
And when I saw his head, I ran away.
And that was the end of that.
So I-- there were no partisan squads.
We survived without them.
And there was a woman that she's here.
She's not from my place, she was in the partisans.
Her brother they wouldn't take.
Her they took.
And so-- partisans, the Russians-- first thing the Russian partisans in our district were afraid because they didn't want to fight the Ukrainians.
And the Ukrainians were killing them.
So they had to fight.
And the one-- the big partisans that went through, that was called [NON-ENGLISH], I heard.
It was a whole big partisan.
And he went all the way to the [NON-ENGLISH], and they killed a lot of them there, the Germans.
No, not us.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
And then, like I said, the first of May, we left.
We left Malynska.
And why we left Malynska?
Because there was no future for the Jew to live there.
And we went, and we wanted to go to Israel, Palestine.
That was the only thing is, is just to go to Israel.
And on the way, they gave us a train, a freight train again.
We went in the freight train.
And there was about 20, 25 Jewish people in the train.
And we came to Chelm.
We didn't find Moniek.
Then we went.
And the train was going back and forth.
The front was still not finished, too.
There were several places with Germans.
And we came to Cracow.
But we heard that the Polish people-- there was Polish on the train.
The whole train was mostly Polish people from different villages who were still on remain.
And they said, we have to kill the Jews, we don't want the Jews to live in where we go to live with us.
So we came off of the train in Cracow.
When they stopped the train in Cracow, we jumped the train.
And the train ran away.
And in Cracow, we didn't know what to do, where to go.
At the station, we met a Jewish man, [NON-ENGLISH].
And he told us to go [NON-ENGLISH] 38.
There was a place where Jewish people get together.
So we went there.
Just me and my mother.
My brother didn't want to come with us onto the train.
He left in Malynska.
He went to [NON-ENGLISH].
What did he do?
I don't know.
But he didn't come with us.
How old was-- how old were you at this time?
I was 16.
Yeah.
And he was 14.
Oh, yeah, he went on his own.
On his own-- I don't know, maybe another guy went with him or whatever.
He remained.
And when we came to [NON-ENGLISH] 38, my mother was very sick.
She had a boil on the back of her head.
They said because she didn't eat salt for all those years.
I don't know.
She was very sick.
They operated on her, they put the thing out.
There was a very bad odor.
And she stayed on the second-- the hospital, I think, was on the second floor.
The children were on the third floor.
Yeah.
And they told me to go on the third floor.
Over there, there was children.
Yes.
